name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ وَٱلْمَجُوسَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (١٧)
```

```english
Indeed Allah will indeed judge between the faithful, the Jews, the Sabaeans, the Christians, the Magians and the polytheists on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed Allah is witness to all things. (17)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَسْجُدُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ وَٱلنُّجُومُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ وَٱلشَّجَرُ وَٱلدَّوَآبُّ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۗ وَمَن يُهِنِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن مُّكْرِمٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۩ (١٨)
```

```english
Have you not regarded that whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth prostrates to Allah, as well as the sun, the moon, and the stars, the mountains, the trees, and the animals and many humans? And many have come to deserve the punishment. Whomever Allah humiliates will find no one who may bring him honour. Indeed Allah does whatever He wishes. (18)
```

```arabic
۞ هَـٰذَانِ خَصْمَانِ ٱخْتَصَمُوا۟ فِى رَبِّهِمْ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ قُطِّعَتْ لَهُمْ ثِيَابٌ مِّن نَّارٍ يُصَبُّ مِن فَوْقِ رُءُوسِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيمُ (١٩)
```

```english
These two contending groups contend concerning their Lord. As for those who are faithless, cloaks of fire will be cut out for them, and boiling water will be poured over their heads, (19)
```

```arabic
يُصْهَرُ بِهِۦ مَا فِى بُطُونِهِمْ وَٱلْجُلُودُ (٢٠)
```

```english
with which their skins and entrails will fuse, (20)
```

```arabic
وَلَهُم مَّقَـٰمِعُ مِنْ حَدِيدٍ (٢١)
```

```english
and there will be clubs of iron for them. (21)
```

```arabic
كُلَّمَآ أَرَادُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِنْ غَمٍّ أُعِيدُوا۟ فِيهَا وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (٢٢)
```

```english
Whenever they desire to leave it out of anguish, they will be turned back into it [and told]: ‘Taste the punishment of the burning!’ (22)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَلُؤْلُؤًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُهُمْ فِيهَا حَرِيرٌ (٢٣)
```

```english
Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them, adorned therein with bracelets of gold and pearl, and their dress therein will be silk. (23)
```

```arabic
وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيِّبِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (٢٤)
```

```english
They were guided to chaste belief, and guided to the path of the All-laudable. (24)
```

 thought
## (Commentary)

After mentioning in the previous verses the differences among people and their disputation concerning Allah—ranging from the straying follower who disputes about Allah without knowledge, to the misguiding leader who disputes about Allah without knowledge, to the waverer who worships Allah on the fringe, and finally those who believe in Allah and perform righteous deeds—He mentions in these verses that Allah is a witness over them and will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection. On that day, they will be humbled and subdued before Him, prostrating in reality before His greatness and majesty, even if some of them refuse to prostrate to Him outwardly in this world; these are the ones for whom the punishment has become due. He then mentions the reward of the believers and the recompense of others after the final judgment on the Day of Resurrection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالصَّابِئِينَ وَالنَّصارى وَالْمَجُوسَ وَالَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** — "Indeed those who believe, and those who are Jews, and the Sabaeans, the Christians, the Magians and the polytheists—indeed Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection" (22:17), etc. By the context of contrast, "those who believe" (الذين آمنوا) refers to those who believed in Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and whose book is the Qur'an.

"Those who are Jews" (الذين هادوا) are the believers in Moses and the messengers before him who adhered to his law, and their book is the Torah. Nebuchadnezzar, the King of Babylon, burned it when he conquered them in the mid-seventh century BCE, and they lost it for a period. Then Ezra the Scribe rewrote it for them in the early sixth century BCE when Cyrus, the King of Persia, conquered Babylon, and the Children of Israel were released from captivity and returned to the Holy Land.

The "Sabaeans" (الصابئون) does not refer to the star-worshippers among the pagans, as evidenced by the contrast in the verse between them and "those who associate partners with Allah" (الذين أشركوا). Rather, they are—according to what has been said—a people intermediate between Judaism and Magianism. They possess a book they attribute to the Prophet Yahya ibn Zakariyya (John the Baptist), and an individual among them is called a "Sabi" by the common folk today. Mention of them has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالنَّصارى وَالصَّابِئِينَ** — "Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans" (2:62).

The "Christians" (النصارى) are the believers in the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary عليه‌السلام, and the prophets before him. Their sacred books are the four Gospels of Luke, Mark, Matthew, and John, and the books of the Old Testament as recognized and sanctified by the Church. However, the Qur'an mentions that their book is the Gospel (الإنجيل) revealed to Jesus عليه‌السلام.

The "Magians" (المجوس) are known to be the followers of Zoroaster, and their sacred book is the "Avesta." However, the history of his life and the time of his appearance are extremely obscure, as if his reports have been severed. They lost the book upon Alexander's conquest of Persia, and it was later rewritten during the time of the Sassanid kings. Consequently, it became difficult to ascertain the core of their doctrine. What is certain is that they affirm two principles for the governance of the world: a principle of good and a principle of evil—Yazdan and Ahriman, or Light and Darkness. They sanctify the angels and seek proximity to them without taking idols for them like the pagans. They also sanctify the elemental simple substances, especially fire, and they had fire temples in Persia, China, India, and elsewhere. They trace everything back to "Ahura Mazda," the originator of all.

"Those who associate partners with Allah" (الذين أشركوا) are the pagans, the idol-worshippers. The foundations of their doctrines are three: Sabaean paganism, Brahmanism, and Buddhism. There were other peoples who worshipped whatever idols they wished in whatever manner they chose, without basing it on an organized foundation, such as the Arabs of the Hijaz and various tribes on the fringes of the inhabited world. A detailed discussion concerning them has already preceded in the tenth volume of this book.

His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** — "indeed Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection" (22:17). What is meant is the finality of judgment (فصل القضاء) regarding that which the followers of these religions differed and disputed. Thus, the one on the side of truth will be separated and distinguished from the one on the side of falsehood—a separation and distinction that no cover can hide and no barrier can obstruct.

The repetition of "indeed" (إن) in the verse is for emphasis (التأكيد), necessitated by the long separation between the first "indeed" (إن) at the beginning of the verse and its predicate (خبرها). Similar to this is His saying: **ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا ثُمَّ جاهَدُوا وَصَبَرُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — "Then indeed your Lord—to those who migrated after they were persecuted, then waged jihad and were patient—indeed your Lord is thereafter all-forgiving, all-merciful" (16:110), and His saying: **ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلذِينَ عَمِلُوا السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — "Then indeed your Lord—to those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent after that and reform—indeed your Lord is thereafter all-forgiving, all-merciful" (16:119).

His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ** — "Indeed Allah is witness to all things" (22:17) is a justification for the judgment—that it is a judgment based on truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يَسْجُدُ لَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَالشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ وَالنُّجُومُ وَالْجِبالُ وَالشَّجَرُ وَالدَّوَابُّ** — "Have you not regarded that to Allah prostrates whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth, and the sun, the moon, and the stars, the mountains, the trees, and the animals" (22:18) to the end of the verse. It appears that the address is to everyone who sees and is fit to be addressed, and "regarding" (الرؤية) here means knowledge (العلم). It is also possible that it is specific to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, in which case "regarding" refers to heart-vision (الرؤية القلبية), as He said concerning him: **ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى أَفَتُمارُونَهُ عَلى ما يَرى** — "The heart did not belie what it saw. Will you then dispute with him about what he saw?" (53:11-12).

The generalization of prostration (السجدة) to include things like the sun, moon, stars, and mountains—which are not among the possessors of intellect—is evidence that what is meant is existential prostration (السجدة التكوينية). This is the humility and lowliness (التذلل والصغار) before His might and majesty, the Exalted, and under His subdual and sovereignty. This necessitates that "whoever is on the earth" (مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ) includes the human species, both believer and disbeliever, as there is no exception in existential prostration and ontological humility.

The omission of the heavens and the earth themselves from the list of prostrators, despite the ruling applying to them in reality, indicates that the meaning of the speech is that all creatures, celestial and terrestrial, whether possessing intellect or not, are prostrating to Allah, humbled in their existence before His might and majesty; they never cease to prostrate to Him, the Exalted, with an existential, involuntary prostration (سجودا تكوينيا اضطراريا).

His saying: **وَكَثِيرٌ مِنَ النَّاسِ** — "and many of the people" (22:18) is conjoined to "whoever is in the heavens" (مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ), etc. That is: and many of the people prostrate to Him. Attributing prostration to "many of the people" after it was already included in the previous phrase for all of them is evidence that this prostration is of a different type than the former, even if they share the root meaning of humility. This type is legislative, voluntary prostration (السجود التشريعي الاختياري) by falling upon the ground as a representation of the existential, involuntary prostration and as a manifestation of the meaning of servitude (العبودية).

His saying: **وَكَثِيرٌ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ الْعَذابُ** — "and many for whom the punishment has become due" (22:18). The contrast between this and the preceding phrase indicates that its meaning is: and many of them refuse to prostrate. He placed in its stead that which is its necessary consequence and result, which is the establishment of punishment for the one who waxes proud against Allah and refuses to submit to Him, the Exalted. The establishment of punishment was placed in the position of the refusal to prostrate to indicate that it is their own deed returning to them, and to serve as a preamble to His subsequent saying: **وَمَنْ يُهِنِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ مُكْرِمٍ** — "and whomever Allah abases, there is no one to honor him" (22:18), which indicates that the establishment of punishment for them following their refusal to prostrate is an abasement and disgrace (هوان وخزي) that attaches to them, after which there is no honor or good.

Thus, their refusal to prostrate entails, by the will of Allah the Exalted, the establishment of punishment for them, which is an abasement after which there is never any honoring, for all good is in the hand of Allah, as He said: **بِيَدِكَ الْخَيْرُ** — "All good is in Your hand" (3:26). If He withholds it from someone, there is no one else to grant it.

His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْعَلُ ما يَشاءُ** — "Indeed Allah does whatever He wishes" (22:18) is an allusion to the universality of power (عموم القدرة) and a justification for what preceded regarding the establishment of punishment for those who wax proud against prostrating to Him and their abasement—an abasement followed by no honor.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that Allah will distinguish on the Day of Resurrection between those who differ. For you know that all celestial and terrestrial beings submit and humble themselves to Him existentially (تكوينا), but among humans, there are those who manifest humility and submission to Him in the station of servitude, and those who wax proud against that. These are the ones for whom the punishment has become due, and Allah has abased them with an abasement followed by no honor; He is powerful over whatever He wishes, the Doer of what He intends. From this, the connection of the verse to what preceded it becomes clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هذانِ خَصْمانِ اخْتَصَمُوا فِي رَبِّهِمْ فَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا قُطِّعَتْ لَهُمْ ثِيابٌ مِنْ نارٍ يُصَبُّ مِنْ فَوْقِ رُؤُسِهِمُ الْحَمِيمُ** — "These two are adversaries who dispute concerning their Lord. As for those who are faithless, garments of fire will be cut out for them, and boiling water will be poured over their heads" (22:19). The demonstration "these two" (هذانِ) refers to the two groups indicated by His previous saying: "indeed Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection" and His subsequent saying: "and many of the people, and many for whom the punishment has become due."

It is known from the restriction of the disputants—despite the multiplicity of their religions and sects—into two adversaries (خصمين) that they are all divided into those on the side of truth and those on the side of falsehood. For were it not for truth and falsehood, the various creeds and sects, in all their divergence, would never be restricted to two. The one on the side of truth and the one on the side of falsehood are the believer in the truth and the disbeliever in it. Thus, these groups, despite the divergence of their views, are restricted to two adversaries. And despite their restriction to two adversaries, they have views numbering more than two; how beautiful is His expression: **خَصْمانِ اخْتَصَمُوا** — "two adversaries who dispute" (using the plural verb for the dual noun), where He did not say "adversaries who dispute" (plural noun) nor "two adversaries who both dispute" (dual verb).

He made their dispute "concerning their Lord" (في ربهم), meaning they differed regarding the description of His Lordship (ربوبيته), the Exalted. To the description of Lordship return all differences of religions, no matter how far they reach. They are between the one who describes his Lord with the names and attributes He deserves and the acts that befit Him—thus believing in what he has described, which is the truth, and acting according to what His description necessitates, which is righteous deeds; this is the believer who performs righteous deeds—and the one who does not describe Him with the names and attributes He deserves, such as the one who affirms for Him a partner or a son, thereby denying His oneness, or attributes creation and origination to nature or time, or denies prophethood or the message of some of the messengers, or a necessity among the necessities of the true religion—thus disbelieving in the truth and covering it; this is the disbeliever. So the believer in his Lord and the disbeliever are the two adversaries in the sense mentioned.

Then He began describing the recompense of the two adversaries and explained the end of each of their affairs after the finality of judgment. He presented those who disbelieved first, saying: **فَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا قُطِّعَتْ لَهُمْ ثِيابٌ مِنْ نارٍ يُصَبُّ مِنْ فَوْقِ رُؤُسِهِمُ الْحَمِيمُ** — "As for those who are faithless, garments of fire will be cut out for them, and boiling water will be poured over their heads" (22:19), meaning hot, boiling water.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُصْهَرُ بِهِ ما فِي بُطُونِهِمْ وَالْجُلُودُ** — "with it will be melted what is in their bellies and [their] skins" (22:20). Melting (الصهر) is liquefaction; that is, what is in their bellies of intestines and their skins will melt and be cooked by that boiling water.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَهُمْ مَقامِعُ مِنْ حَدِيدٍ** — "and for them are maces of iron" (22:21). Maces (المقامع) is the plural of *miqma'ah*, which is a mallet or a club.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلَّما أَرادُوا أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها مِنْ غَمٍّ أُعِيدُوا فِيها وَذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ** — "Whenever they desire to leave it out of anguish, they will be turned back into it, [and told]: ‘Taste the punishment of the burning!’" (22:22). The pronoun in "from it" (مِنْها) refers to the Fire, and "out of anguish" (مِنْ غَمٍّ) is an explanation for it, or the "from" (من) denotes causality. "Burning" (الحريق) means that which burns, like "painful" (الأليم) means that which causes pain.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ اللهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** — "Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith" (22:23) to the end of the verse. "Bracelets" (الأساور) is said to be the plural of *aswirah*, which is the plural of *siwar* (bracelet), and according to al-Raghib, it is the Arabized form of the Persian *dastwarah*. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُدُوا إِلَى الطَّيِّبِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَهُدُوا إِلى صِراطِ الْحَمِيدِ** — "They were guided to pure speech, and they were guided to the path of the All-laudable" (22:24). "Pure" (الطيب) speech is that in which there is no foulness, and foul speech is falsehood in all its types. All pure speech has been gathered in His saying, the Exalted, reporting about them: **دَعْواهُمْ فِيها سُبْحانَكَ اللهُمَّ وَتحيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ وَآخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — "Their call therein will be, ‘O Allah! Immaculate are You!’ and their greeting therein will be, ‘Peace!’ and their last call will be, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’" (10:10). Thus, their being guided to pure speech is its being made easy for them. Their being guided to the path of the All-laudable (الحميد)—and the All-laudable is one of the names of Allah, the Exalted—means that nothing issues from them except praiseworthy action, just as nothing issues from them except pure speech.

Between this verse and His saying: **كُلَّما أَرادُوا أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها مِنْ غَمٍّ أُعِيدُوا فِيها وَذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ** — "Whenever they desire to leave it out of anguish, they will be turned back into it, [and told]: ‘Taste the punishment of the burning!’" (22:22), there is a clear contrast.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of narration from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from Ali عليه‌السلام in a tradition: He عليه‌السلام said: "Ask me before you lose me." Thereupon al-Ash'ath ibn Qays stood up and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, how is the tribute (الجزية) taken from the Magians when no book was revealed to them and no prophet was sent to them?" He said: **بلى يا أشعث قد أنزل الله إليهم كتابا وبعث إليهم رسولا ـ حتى كان لهم ملك سكر ذات ليلة ـ فدعا بابنته إلى فراشه فارتكبها. فلما أصبح تسامع به قومه فاجتمعوا إلى بابه ـ فقالوا : أيها الملك دنست علينا ديننا وأهلكته ـ فاخرج نطهرك ونقيم عليك الحد فقال لهم : اجتمعوا واسمعوا قولي فإن يكن لي مخرج مما ارتكبت ـ وإلا فشأنكم فاجتمعوا فقال لهم : هل علمتم أن الله لم يخلق خلقا أكرم عليه من أبينا آدم وأمنا حواء؟ قالوا : صدقت أيها الملك قال : أوليس قد زوج بنيه بناته وبناته من بنيه؟ قالوا : صدقت هذا هو الدين ـ فتعاقدوا على ذلك فمحا الله ما في صدورهم من العلم ـ ورفع عنهم الكتاب فهم الكفرة يدخلون النار بلا حساب ـ والمنافقون أشد حالا منهم.** — "Yes, O Ash'ath, Allah did reveal a book to them and sent a messenger to them—until they had a king who became intoxicated one night, called his daughter to his bed, and committed incest with her. When morning came, his people heard of it and gathered at his door, saying: 'O King, you have defiled our religion for us and destroyed it; come out so we may purify you and carry out the legal punishment upon you.' He said to them: 'Gather and hear my words; if there is a way out for me from what I committed, then so be it, otherwise, do as you will.' They gathered, and he said to them: 'Do you not know that Allah did not create any creation more noble to Him than our father Adam and our mother Eve?' They said: 'You have spoken the truth, O King.' He said: 'Did he not marry his sons to his daughters and his daughters to his sons?' They said: 'You have spoken the truth, this is the religion.' So they agreed upon that, and Allah erased the knowledge from their hearts and took away the book from them. Thus they are the disbelievers who enter the Fire without reckoning—and the hypocrites are in a worse state than them." Al-Ash'ath said: "By Allah, I have never heard an answer like this; by Allah, I shall never return to the like of it again."

**I say:** His saying: "and the hypocrites are in a worse state than them" contains an allusion to al-Ash'ath. Regarding the Magians being among the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), there are other narrations stating that they had a prophet whom they killed and a book which they burned.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْعَلُ ما يَشاءُ** — "Indeed Allah does whatever He wishes" (22:18): Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Lalika'i in *al-Sunnah*, and al-Khala'i in his *Fawa'id* narrated from Ali: that it was said to him: "There is a man here who speaks about the Will (المشيئة)." Ali said to him: **يا عبد الله خلقك الله لما يشاء أو لما شئت؟ قال بل لما يشاء قال : فيمرضك إذا شاء أو إذا شئت؟ قال : بل إذا شاء. قال : فيشفيك إذا شاء أو إذا شئت؟ قال بل إذا شاء. قال فيدخلك الجنة حيث شاء أو حيث شئت؟ قال : بل حيث شاء. قال : والله لو قلت غير ذلك ـ لضربت الذي فيه عيناك بالسيف.** — "O servant of Allah, did Allah create you for what He wishes or for what you wished?" He said: "Rather, for what He wishes." He said: "Does He make you ill when He wishes or when you wish?" He said: "Rather, when He wishes." He said: "Does He heal you when He wishes or when you wish?" He said: "Rather, when He wishes." He said: "Does He admit you into Paradise where He wishes or where you wish?" He said: "Rather, where He wishes." He said: "By Allah, had you said otherwise, I would have struck the place of your eyes with the sword."

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Tawhid*, with its chain from Abdullah ibn al-Maymun al-Qaddah from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father عليه‌السلام, and in it: "He admits you where He wishes or where you wish," without mentioning Paradise. A narration with this meaning has already preceded, which we explained in the commentary on His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — "and He does not mislead by it any except the transgressors" (2:26) in the first volume of this book.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain to Sulayman ibn Ja'far al-Ja'fari, who said: al-Rida عليه‌السلام said: **المشية من صفات الأفعال ـ فمن زعم أن الله لم يزل مريدا شائيا فليس بموحد.** — "The Will (المشية) is among the attributes of action (صفات الأفعال); so whoever claims that Allah has never ceased to be intending and willing is not a monotheist."

**I say:** In his saying عليه‌السلام secondly: "never ceased to be intending and willing" (لم يزل مريدا شائيا) there is an allusion to the unity of Intention (الإرادة) and Will (المشية), and it is indeed so. For Will is a meaning by which a human is described when considering him as an agent conscious of his act attributed to him. When his agency is complete such that the act is inseparable from him, this very meaning is called Intention. In any case, it is an attribute external to the Essence (الذات) that occurs upon it. Therefore, He, the Exalted, is not described by it as He is described by His essential attributes (صفاته الذاتية) like Knowledge and Power, due to His transcendence above the change of the Essence by the occurrence of accidents. Rather, it is among the attributes of His action, abstracted from the act itself or from the presence of its causes.

Thus, our saying: "Allah intended such-and-such" means that He did it knowing it was most beneficial, or that He prepared its causes knowing it was most beneficial. If it were in the sense it exists in us—other than the Essence—then if it were said: "Allah has never ceased to be intending," it would necessitate affirming something eternal other than Him that was not created by Him, which is contrary to His monotheism (توحيده). As for the one who says: "The meaning of Intention is the knowledge of what is most beneficial, and knowledge is among the attributes of the Essence, so He has never ceased to be intending, i.e., knowing that what He did was most beneficial," this is a reduction of Intention to Knowledge. There is no harm in this, except that counting Intention in this case as another attribute beyond Life, Knowledge, and Power has no justification.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* narrated from Abu Dharr: that he used to swear an oath that this verse—**هذانِ خَصْمانِ اخْتَصَمُوا فِي رَبِّهِمْ** to His saying **إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْعَلُ ما يُرِيدُ**—was revealed concerning the three and the three who engaged in single combat on the day of Badr: they were Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, Ubaydah ibn al-Harith, and Ali ibn Abi Talib—and Utbah and Shaybah, the sons of Rabi'ah, and al-Walid ibn Utbah.

Ali said: **أنا أول من يجثو للخصومة ـ على ركبتيه بين يدي الله يوم القيامة.** — "I am the first who shall kneel for the dispute upon his knees before Allah on the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** It was also narrated therein from several authors of the *Jawami'* from Qays ibn Sa'd ibn Ubadah, Ibn Abbas, and others. It was also narrated in *Majma' al-Bayan* from Abu Dharr and Ata.

In *al-Khisal*, from al-Nadr ibn Malik, who said: I said to al-Husayn ibn Ali عليه‌السلام: "O Abu Abdillah, tell me about His saying, the Exalted: **هذانِ خَصْمانِ اخْتَصَمُوا فِي رَبِّهِمْ**." He said: **نحن وبنو أمية اختصمنا في الله تعالى : قلنا صدق الله ، وقالوا : كذب ، فنحن الخصمان يوم القيامة.** — "We and the Umayyads disputed concerning Allah the Exalted: we said, 'Allah has spoken the truth,' and they said, 'He has lied.' So we are the two adversaries on the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** This is of the nature of application (الجري), and similar to it is what is in *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Ibn Abi Hamzah from al-Baqir عليه‌السلام: "As for those who disbelieved in the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali عليه‌السلام, garments of fire will be cut out for them."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: **وَهُدُوا إِلَى الطَّيِّبِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ** — He said: "Monotheism (التوحيد) and sincerity (الإخلاص)." **وَهُدُوا إِلى صِراطِ الْحَمِيدِ** — He said: "The guardianship (الولاية)."

**I say:** In *al-Mahasin*, with its chain from Durays from al-Baqir عليه‌السلام, there is something of similar meaning.

In *al-Majma'*, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said: **ما أحد أحب إليه الحمد من الله عز ذكره.** — "There is no one to whom praise is more beloved than Allah, exalted is His mention."
